segments.s030-g17: This is a noun phrase whose head is منزل used in the sense 'stage/stop' of a journey; دگر modifies منزل and means 'another'/'a further'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' (definite), and شاه آزادمرد is the noun plus epithet 'king, the noble man' describing whose stop it is. In poetry the ezāfe linking these words is omitted, so the whole phrase functions as the object/complement of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s030-g18: لب دجله و شهر بغداد are two locative noun phrases ('on the bank of the Tigris' and 'the city of Baghdad') coordinated by و 'and'; the final کرد is the past‑tense 3rd person singular of کردن 'to do/make' completing the verbal phrase منزل کردن ('to make a stop/encamp'). Poetic style omits prepositions like در, so لب دجله here means 'on the bank of the Tigris.'
segments.s030-g1: فریدون (subject) + به خورشید (directional prepositional phrase) + بر برد سر is a compact poetic clause: بر here acts as a prefixed directional/verb particle and برد is the past action 'took/lifted' (poetic/past). The phrase means 'Fereydun lifted/turned his head toward the sun.' Word order is Subject — (prepositional phrase) — verb + object (سر).
segments.s030-g2: کمر تنگ بستش is an idiomatic, semi‑reflexive expression: کمر (waist / girdle) + تنگ (tight) + بستش (past 'bound/fastened' with enclitic -ش marking the object/reflexive). Together 'he girded his waist tightly' (prepared himself). به کین پدر is a motive clause introduced by به (here 'for / out of') followed by کین پدر 'his father's vengeance' explaining why he girded himself.
segments.s030-g3: برون‌رفت functions as a poetic verbal noun/past action 'came forth / emerged' (here treated as 'came forth'); خرم is the quality 'joyful' or 'joy' acting as subject/predicate. به خرداد روز is a temporal adverbial 'on the day of Khordad.' Word order places the temporal phrase after the verb-phrase in a compressed poetic style.
segments.s030-g4: به marks accompaniment/instrumentality here ('with' / 'under'); نیک اختر 'a lucky/auspicious star' and فال گیتی فروز is a compound: فال 'omen' + گیتی فروز 'world‑illuminating' (فروز as adjective/verb meaning 'brighten'). The whole phrase describes the favorable astral/omen conditions accompanying the emergence.
segments.s030-g5: سپاه (subject 'the army') + انجمن شد ('became an assembly' — شد is past of شدن 'to become', turning سپاه into a gathered council). به درگاه او is a locative phrase 'at his court/threshold' showing where the assembly occurred.
segments.s030-g6: به ابر اندر آمد = 'into/over him a cloud came in' (به + noun + اندر آمد is a compact way to say 'a cloud entered / appeared'). سر گاه او is a locative/positional phrase 'over his head/seat' (سر = top/summit; گاه here marks place/time), so the clause means 'a cloud came over his throne/seat.'
segments.s030-g7: به introduces instrumental means 'by/with'; پیلان (elephants) is followed by the epithet گردون‌کش (literally 'heaven‑slayer' / 'sky‑puller') — a poetic epithet attached to elephants — then و گاومیش 'and buffaloes.' The whole phrase lists the beasts used as conveyances.
segments.s030-g8: سپه را uses را to mark the definite/affected object 'the army'; همی before the verb is a classical progressive/emphatic particle ('was/indeed' — here giving continuous or emphatic sense). توشه 'provisions' is the direct object of بردند (past plural 'they carried/took'), and پیش ('before/in the presence of') indicates direction/place — 'they brought provisions forward before him/for the army.' Word order places the agent/instruments phrase before the topicalized object and verb.
segments.s030-g9: This is a noun phrase with two coordinated items (کیانوش و پرمایه). و simply joins the two names. بر دست شاه is a prepositional phrase placed after the names: بر + دست (literally “on/at the hand”) + شاه (possessor), an idiom meaning “at the king's side / under the king’s protection.” Word order is name(s) → locative phrase.
segments.s030-g10: چو here = 'like/as'. کهتر برادر is a comparative phrase 'a younger/lesser brother' (کهتر modifies برادر). ورا is the unstressed third‑person object pronoun 'to him' and precedes the predicate نیک‌خواه, which is a predicative noun/adjective meaning 'well‑wisher' or 'benevolent toward him'. The whole clause functions as a simile/comment on the relation to the king.
segments.s030-g11: همی رفت is a habitual/continuous past expression: همی (continuous/emphatic adverb) + رفت (past of رفتن) = 'kept going'. منزل به منزل is a repetitive adverbial phrase 'from dwelling/stage to dwelling/stage' (motion phrase). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying the motion. Word order is adverbial + verb + directional phrase + simile.
segments.s030-g12: This is a paired nominal clause with parallel 'X full of Y' constructions. سری پر ز کینه = 'a head full of hatred' (سری = head, پر ز = 'full of', کینه = 'hatred'). دلی پر ز داد = 'a heart full of justice' (دلی here functions as 'heart/heart‑like' and is modified by پر ز داد). ز is the poetic/prepositional form meaning 'of/from'; داد is the noun 'justice' rather than a verbal form. The two halves are balanced for contrast.
segments.s030-g13: به اروندرود = 'toward the Arvand (River)' (به + proper name). اندر آورد is a compound verbal idea here: اندر (in/toward) + آورد (past of آوردن) meaning 'brought/turned (into)'; together with روی (face) it means 'turned (his) face toward'. روی is the object 'face.' Verb follows the small prepositional particle اندر and takes روی as its direct object.
segments.s030-g14: چنان چون is a redundant poetic intensifier meaning 'such/just so.' بود is the past copula 'was.' مرد is the subject 'man.' دیهیم‌جوی is a compound predicate noun meaning 'seeker of diyah (blood‑money),' i.e. an avenger. The clause states his character (he was such a man).
segments.s030-g15: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پهلوانی is an abstract noun 'heroism / valour' and is the complement of ندانی. ندانی is the negative present/pastless form 'you do not know' (classical usage: 'you know not'). زبان ('tongue/language') is fronted here and will be specified by the next phrase (به تازی), so it functions adverbially ('in the tongue of ...').
segments.s030-g16: به تازی = 'in the Tazi/Arabic tongue' (به = 'in/by', تازی = 'Arabic/Arab'). تو is the addressed 'you' (subject or vocative). اروند را is the definite object marked by را (اروند = Arvand river). دجله is the Arabic name 'Dijla/Tigris' that the addressee is told to apply. خوان is here a verbal form meaning 'call/name' (an imperative/indicative jussive in classical usage: 'you call'), so the clause reads 'you call Arvand Dajleh in Arabic.'

sentences.s030-s9: That noble king made another halt on the bank of the Tigris and in the city of Baghdad.
sentences.s030-s1: Fereydun lifted his head toward the sun and girded his waist tightly for his father's vengeance.
sentences.s030-s2: Joy came forth on the day of Khordad, under a fortunate star and with an omen that lit the world.
sentences.s030-s3: The army became an assembly at his court; a cloud came in above his seat.
sentences.s030-s4: By elephants — heaven‑lifters — and buffaloes they carried provisions forward for the army.
sentences.s030-s5: Kianoush and Parmāya stood at the king's side, like a younger brother and a well-wisher to him.
sentences.s030-s6: He kept going from place to place like the wind, his head full of hatred, his heart full of justice.
sentences.s030-s7: He turned his face toward the Arvand River; such was he — a man bent on seeking blood‑money.
sentences.s030-s8: If you do not know heroism, then in the Arabic tongue you may call the Arvand the Tigris.
